title: The War That Almost Ended Rome
subtitle: The Punic Wars, 264–146 BC
tagline: For a hundred years, Rome and Carthage fought for control of the world. Carthage produced the greatest general who ever lived. Rome almost didn't survive meeting him.
villain_mandate: Carthage and Hannibal must feel completely dominant. Hannibal destroyed Rome's armies three times in a row. His tactical genius at Cannae is still studied in war colleges. Rome's survival must feel miraculous.

scenes.title: Two Empires
scenes.narration: 264 BC. Two cities controlled the Mediterranean world. Rome controlled Italy. Carthage — in modern Tunisia — controlled the sea. The richest trading empire in history. The largest navy ever built. They had coexisted for centuries. Then they went to war over Sicily. The war they started would last one hundred and eighteen years and nearly destroy both of them.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Split view — Rome on one side, Carthage on the other. A map of the Mediterranean between them, each controlling different coasts. Carthaginian warships in the harbor, massive and sleek. Roman legions on Italian roads. Two civilizations that have never needed to fight, about to discover they cannot share the sea. 16:9.

scenes.title: Rome Builds a Navy in 60 Days
scenes.narration: Rome had no navy. Carthage had the greatest navy in the world. Rome found a wrecked Carthaginian warship on a beach and built two hundred copies in sixty days. They couldn't match Carthaginian sailors, so they invented a boarding bridge — the corvus — that turned sea battles into land battles. Rome won the First Punic War by refusing to fight on Carthage's terms.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Roman workers copying a Carthaginian warship hull in a vast shipyard — two hundred ships being built simultaneously. The corvus boarding bridge shown deploying: a spiked bridge crashing onto a Carthaginian deck, Roman legionaries storming across. Carthaginian sailors facing Roman soldiers — the wrong kind of fight. 16:9.

scenes.title: Hannibal's Oath
scenes.narration: Carthage lost the First Punic War and paid a crippling indemnity. A young Carthaginian boy, nine years old, watched his father leave for Spain and begged to come along. His father agreed — if the boy would swear an oath of eternal enmity to Rome. The boy swore. His name was Hannibal Barca. He would spend the next thirty years making good on that oath.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. A nine-year-old Hannibal before an altar, swearing his oath — small, fierce, completely serious. His father Hamilcar Barca watching. Then: TIME JUMP — Hannibal as a young general in Spain, building an army, his father's legacy completed. The face of someone who has been preparing for one thing his entire life. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Impossible March
scenes.narration: 218 BC. Hannibal did what Rome considered physically impossible. He marched forty thousand soldiers, nine thousand cavalry, and thirty-seven war elephants over the Alps. In November. In snow. Half his army died crossing. He lost one eye to fever in the Italian marshes. He arrived in Italy with twenty-six thousand men, half-frozen, and immediately destroyed every Roman army he encountered.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Hannibal's army in the Alps — soldiers, horses, and war elephants struggling through mountain passes in deep snow. The scale of the suffering: men and animals falling, the path barely visible. Then: the other side — Italy spread below them, the prize. Hannibal, one eye lost to infection, looking down at Roman territory. Half his force is gone. He is still smiling. 16:9.

scenes.title: Three Roman Armies Destroyed
scenes.narration: Hannibal defeated Rome at the Trebia River. Then at Lake Trasimene — the largest ambush in ancient history, fifteen thousand Romans killed in three hours. Rome sent its largest army ever assembled. Eighty thousand men. Hannibal had fifty thousand. The battle was at Cannae. It lasted one afternoon. Seventy thousand Romans died. No army in history had ever been so completely destroyed.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. THREE BATTLE SEQUENCE — Trebia River: Roman legions surprised, water running red. Lake Trasimene: Romans marching into fog, Hannibal's forces on the hillside above them, the ambush springing. Cannae: the double envelopment visible from above — eighty thousand Romans surrounded, compressed, destroyed. The same Roman eagle standard falling three times. 16:9.

scenes.title: Rome Will Not Negotiate
scenes.narration: After Cannae, every city in the ancient world expected Rome to surrender. Hannibal's envoys arrived in Rome with the rings of dead senators — proof of the scale of the slaughter. The Roman Senate refused to ransom prisoners. Refused to negotiate. Refused to acknowledge defeat. They raised new armies. They changed strategy. They would never again fight Hannibal in open battle. They would starve him instead.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The Roman Senate chamber after Cannae — senators in togas, the floor still dusty with the news. Hannibal's envoys presenting the rings of the dead. Every senator knowing what those rings mean. The vote: no negotiation. No surrender. New legions forming outside. Rome deciding to outlast the man who has destroyed three of its armies. 16:9.

scenes.title: Scipio Africanus
scenes.narration: Rome produced its answer to Hannibal: Publius Cornelius Scipio. A young general who had survived Cannae at eighteen. He studied Hannibal's tactics and used them against Carthage itself — taking the war to Africa. He captured Carthage's Spanish allies, cut off Hannibal's supplies, and forced Carthage to recall Hannibal from Italy. Hannibal had spent fifteen years in Italy and never lost a major battle. He was called home to defend against a man he'd never fought.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Scipio Africanus — young, intense, studying captured Carthaginian battle plans. Roman legions boarding ships for Africa. The Carthaginian coast. Hannibal, in Italy, receiving the recall order — looking back at the country he has occupied for fifteen years. He has not been defeated. He is being pulled away. 16:9.

scenes.title: Zama — The Only Defeat
scenes.narration: 202 BC. The Battle of Zama. Hannibal and Scipio met before the battle and talked — two men who had studied each other for years. Then they fought. Scipio turned Hannibal's own double-envelopment against him. It was the only major battle Hannibal ever lost. The Second Punic War was over. Carthage paid Rome a crippling fine and surrendered its navy and its empire. Hannibal, defeated at last, went into exile.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Hannibal and Scipio meeting between the armies before Zama — two generals who respect each other, speaking through interpreters, knowing this is the only way this ends. Then: the battle, Scipio's cavalry returning to envelop Hannibal's formation. Hannibal watching the move he invented being used against him. His expression: acknowledgment. 16:9.

scenes.title: Carthage Must Be Destroyed
scenes.narration: Fifty years later, Carthage had rebuilt. It was prosperous again — no longer a military threat, just a wealthy city. Rome didn't care. Senator Cato ended every speech, on every topic, with the same phrase: Carthago delenda est. Carthage must be destroyed. In 146 BC, Rome besieged Carthage and burned it for seventeen days. They salted the earth. A civilization eight hundred years old was erased.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Carthage burning — the great city, its harbor, its famous temples, all in flames. Roman soldiers moving through the streets. The fire burning for seventeen days: shown as dawn-to-dawn-to-dawn. The harbor — once the greatest in the world — filled with ash. Then: empty ground where a city was. 16:9.

scenes.title: One Hundred Years
scenes.narration: One hundred and eighteen years. Three wars. The greatest general who ever lived. Seventy thousand Romans dead in a single afternoon. And Rome survived it all — not by matching Carthage's genius, but by refusing to stop. They changed the rules. They took the war to Africa. They waited. Some enemies you beat by being impossible to destroy. Subscribe to Gods & Glory for the battles that shaped history.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Final card — the empty site of Carthage: grass growing where the city was, the harbor visible. Then: Rome, still standing, larger than before. Two cities. One survives. The lesson visible in the geography. 16:9.

viral_hook: Hannibal marched thirty-seven war elephants over the Alps in November. In snow. Lost half his army. Arrived in Italy and immediately destroyed three Roman armies. Rome refused to surrender.
highlight_note: Scene 5 — the three-battle sequence. The same Roman eagle falling three times. Visually devastating.
youtube_title: The War That Almost Ended Rome | The Punic Wars | Gods & Glory
lesson: Genius can win every battle and still lose the war. Rome didn't beat Hannibal — they outlasted him.
